updates.q: Six controls for bounding agent autonomy?
updates.a: (1) Action allowlist. (2) Per-conversation spend cap. (3) Max-step budget. (4) Human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions. (5) Per-step observability (every input/output logged). (6) Time/wall-clock limits to prevent runaway loops.

updates.q: Three Anthropic-specific concepts to know before a Claude-shop interview?
updates.a: (1) Constitutional AI / Claude's training approach. (2) The current Responsible Scaling Policy and ASL tiers. (3) The production stack: 1M-token context, prompt caching, extended thinking, and MCP for tools. Skim the latest RSP and Claude docs the week of the interview.

new_cards.q: Typical chunk size and overlap for prose RAG?
new_cards.a: Roughly 500-1000 tokens per chunk with 10-20% overlap is the production default. Too small fragments meaning; too large dilutes the retrieval signal. Always chunk on natural boundaries (paragraph, section) before falling back to a recursive splitter.

new_cards.q: What does a cross-encoder reranker do that a bi-encoder cannot?
new_cards.a: A cross-encoder reads the query and candidate document jointly in one forward pass, so it captures token-level interactions. Bi-encoders embed each side independently and compare with cosine, which is fast but loses fine-grained relevance signal. Use bi-encoders for retrieval at scale, cross-encoders to rerank the top 20-100.

new_cards.q: What is reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) and why is it the hybrid-search default?
new_cards.a: RRF combines results from multiple retrievers by summing 1/(k + rank_i) for each doc across lists. It's score-agnostic, so you can fuse BM25 and dense scores without normalizing. The k=60 default works well out of the box and beats most learned fusion in practice.

new_cards.q: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem in RAG?
new_cards.a: LLMs attend most strongly to context near the start and end of the prompt, and recall items in the middle worst. Put the most relevant retrieved chunks first or last, and keep retrieved context tight - more chunks can hurt accuracy past a threshold.

new_cards.q: How do you pick an embedding model for a new RAG project?
new_cards.a: Run a retrieval eval on a few hundred query-document pairs from your domain, scoring recall@k and MRR. Start with a strong general model (Voyage-3, text-embedding-3-large, BGE-M3) and only move to a domain-specific or fine-tuned embedder if the eval shows a real gap. Latency and self-host requirements then pick the winner.

new_cards.q: HNSW vs IVF index for ANN search -- when to pick which?
new_cards.a: HNSW gives the best recall-latency tradeoff for in-memory corpora up to ~100M vectors and is the default in pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate. IVF (or IVF+PQ) wins when memory cost matters and you can tolerate slightly lower recall - typical for billion-scale corpora in FAISS or Milvus.

new_cards.q: Even with a 1M-token window, why might you still want RAG?
new_cards.a: Per-call cost and latency scale with input tokens even with caching, so stuffing 1M tokens every call is wasteful when most queries need a few KB. RAG also gives you citations, access control per document, and freshness via reindexing - all hard to bolt onto full-context.

new_cards.q: RAG returns correct chunks but the model ignores them -- first thing to check?
new_cards.a: Inspect the actual prompt sent to the model: chunks may be truncated, ordered badly (lost in the middle), or buried under a long system message. Then confirm the system prompt explicitly tells the model to answer only from the provided context and to say 'I don't know' otherwise.

new_cards.q: How do you enforce document-level access control in RAG?
new_cards.a: Filter at the vector DB query time using metadata tags for tenant, ACL group, or document sensitivity - never rely on the LLM to honor 'don't show this user X'. Index the same chunk twice if it has multiple ACLs, and verify with an offline test that a user cannot retrieve another tenant's docs.

new_cards.q: Offline vs online evals -- what does each catch?
new_cards.a: Offline evals (golden sets, replay) catch regressions before deploy and let you compare prompts or models head-to-head. Online evals (thumbs, escalation rate, task completion, regret) catch real-user distribution shift and long-tail failures that the golden set never covers. You need both.

new_cards.q: Four common ways LLM-as-judge lies to you?
new_cards.a: (1) Position bias - prefers the first or last answer. (2) Length bias - rewards longer responses. (3) Self-preference - judges trained on a model rate that model higher. (4) Rubric drift - vague rubrics yield inconsistent grades. Mitigations: randomize order, length-normalize, use a different family as judge, write rubrics with concrete examples.

new_cards.q: How do you bootstrap a golden eval set when you have no labeled data?
new_cards.a: Mine real or synthetic user queries, have a strong model draft answers, then hand-correct 50-200 of them in one sitting. Stratify by intent so each failure mode is represented. Track per-category accuracy from day one - a small high-quality set beats a noisy large one.

new_cards.q: What additional eval dimensions matter for a money-spending agent?
new_cards.a: Beyond task success: false-positive action rate, cost-per-resolved-task, unauthorized-action rate (action outside allowlist), and human-escalation rate. Always include red-team eval cases with adversarial inputs that try to trigger spend, and treat any unauthorized irreversible action as a hard fail.

new_cards.q: Standard sections of a production system prompt?
new_cards.a: Role and goal, operating constraints (what not to do), tool/output schema, retrieved context block clearly delimited, few-shot examples for tricky cases, and a final instruction restating the task. Keep the stable prefix (role, constraints, tools, examples) at the top so prompt caching can reuse it.

new_cards.q: When should you use a reasoning model vs a non-reasoning model + CoT prompt?
new_cards.a: Use a reasoning model (Claude extended thinking, GPT-5 thinking, Gemini 2.5 thinking) for multi-step math, code, planning, or hard tool decisions - the trained reasoning beats prompted CoT and avoids reasoning-leakage in user output. Use a non-reasoning model for short extraction, classification, and chat where the latency and token cost of thinking aren't worth it.

new_cards.q: What's the standard prompt-caching minimum, and how do you maximize hit rate?
new_cards.a: Most providers require ~1024+ tokens of stable prefix to cache. Keep variable content (user input, retrieved chunks) at the END of the prompt, and keep tools, system prompt, and few-shots byte-identical across calls - any whitespace change invalidates the cache.

new_cards.q: Difference between direct and indirect prompt injection?
new_cards.a: Direct: a user types adversarial instructions into the chat ('ignore previous instructions and...'). Indirect: malicious instructions arrive via retrieved content, a webpage the agent browses, or a tool response. Indirect is harder to defend because the user is benign and the payload looks like data.

new_cards.q: When do you actually need an agent instead of a chain?
new_cards.a: When the next step depends on prior tool outputs in a way you can't enumerate up front - investigation, research, multi-step debugging, open-ended planning. If you can draw the DAG of steps before runtime, build a chain; it's cheaper, more debuggable, and easier to eval.

new_cards.q: Top failure modes of tool calling in production?
new_cards.a: (1) Hallucinated tool names or arguments. (2) Wrong types (string vs number). (3) Skipping required args. (4) Calling the right tool with stale context. (5) Infinite tool-call loops. (6) Ignoring the tool result and answering from priors. Mitigations: strict JSON Schema, structured outputs, step budget, and post-tool reflection.

new_cards.q: Why use JSON Schema with strict structured outputs instead of 'return JSON' in the prompt?
new_cards.a: Strict structured outputs constrain decoding to the schema's grammar, so the response is guaranteed parseable and typed - no retries on malformed JSON, no prompt-injection-driven schema escape. It also doubles as a security boundary: an action field of type enum can't be spoofed.

new_cards.q: Two things a well-designed MCP server should always expose?
new_cards.a: (1) A small set of focused tools with clear, typed schemas and human-readable descriptions the model can reason about. (2) Auth and per-tool permission scopes so the host (Claude Desktop, IDE, agent) can enforce least privilege. Resources and prompts are optional extras.

new_cards.q: Three highest-leverage cost levers for an LLM product?
new_cards.a: (1) Prompt caching on stable prefixes - up to 90% off input tokens. (2) Model routing - send easy queries to a cheap small model, escalate only when needed. (3) Output length control - cap max_tokens and prompt for terseness. Batch API and structured outputs (fewer retries) come next.

new_cards.q: How does a model-routing/cascading setup work?
new_cards.a: A cheap classifier or small model handles the request first; a confidence or self-grader decides whether to escalate to the large model. You typically route 60-90% of traffic to the small model and only pay big-model cost on the hard tail. Eval the router separately - a bad router silently degrades quality.

new_cards.q: Decision tree: prompt vs RAG vs fine-tune?
new_cards.a: Start with prompting. If the model lacks domain facts or freshness, add RAG. If it still can't follow a proprietary format, tone, or narrow task at low latency after good prompting, fine-tune. Fine-tuning is rarely the right first move - it's slow to iterate and bakes in mistakes.

new_cards.q: How much data do you actually need to fine-tune an LLM in 2026?
new_cards.a: For supervised fine-tuning of an instruction-tuned model on a narrow task, 500-5000 high-quality examples usually beats 50k noisy ones. LoRA/QLoRA make this cheap. Data quality, schema consistency, and coverage of edge cases matter far more than raw count.

new_cards.q: What is RLHF and where does it sit relative to SFT and DPO?
new_cards.a: SFT trains on (prompt, ideal answer) pairs. RLHF then trains a reward model from human preference pairs and uses PPO to push the policy toward preferred outputs. DPO skips the reward model and optimizes preferences directly - it's simpler and now the common default for preference tuning.

new_cards.q: What is a regret metric in online LLM evals?
new_cards.a: Regret measures how often a user signal indicates the response was wrong or unhelpful - thumbs-down, re-asked question, edit-and-resubmit, escalation to a human, or task abandonment. It's the cheapest, highest-signal online metric because users produce it for free and it tracks real harm rather than vibes.

new_cards.q: Why is human-in-the-loop required for irreversible agent actions?
new_cards.a: Hallucinations and prompt injections are non-zero, and irreversible actions (payments, deletes, external emails) have no undo. A confirmation step on a typed action schema turns a class of catastrophic failures into a UX speed bump - and gives you labeled data on when the agent was wrong.
